macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Framework: When Kohanim Receive the Skin Based on Physical Separation

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Physical Unity as the Determining Factor
Rabbi Chanina's Testimony: The Skin Was Never Burned Separately
Rabbi Akiva's Inference: Discovery Timing Determines Skin Status
The Sages' Objection: Absence of Evidence Is Not Evidence

macro_segments.title: Reconciling the Two Mishnayot: Sprinkling of Blood Versus Flaying

micro_segments.title: The First Mishnah's Standard: Entitlement After Sprinkling of Blood
Abaye's Reconciliation: Both Mishnayot Reflect Rebbi's View
Rava's Alternative: Both Mishnayot Reflect Rabbi Eliezer ben Shimon's View

macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rebbi and Rabbi Eliezer ben Shimon: Does Blood Alone Permit the Skin?

micro_segments.title: Rebbi's Position: Blood Atones for Skin Even When Separated from Meat
Rabbi Eliezer ben Shimon's Position: Blood Cannot Work for Separated Skin
Application to Invalid Meat: Different Principles of Validity

macro_segments.title: Connecting the Debate to Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua on Korban Validity

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua: Blood and Meat Are Interdependent (If No Meat, No Blood)
Rabbi Eliezer: Blood Can Be Sprinkled Even Without Valid Meat

macro_segments.title: Connecting the Debate to Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua on Korban Validity (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Equation: Linking the Two Debates Through Korban Validity

macro_segments.title: The Complicated Status of Rabbi Yehoshua's Position: Lechatchila vs. Bediavad

micro_segments.title: Rashi's Interpretation: A Lechatchila Prohibition with Bediavad Validity
The Mishnah's Case: Blood Sprinkled When Meat Is Invalid
Reconciling the Position: Even Rabbi Yehoshua Agrees Bediavad It Works

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina's Statement Reconsidered: Supporting Rebbi's View

micro_segments.title: Objection: Burned Offerings Are Taken Out—Doesn't Skin Get Burned Too?
Clarification: Referring to Separate Burning After Flaying, Not Intentional Offerings
The Case of Discovery After Flaying: Treifa Found in Internal Organs
Conclusion: Rabbi Chanina Holds Like Rebbi Regarding Flaying as the Critical Factor

macro_segments.title: Applying the Debate to Bechor: Skin Status When Defect Is Discovered After Flaying

micro_segments.title: Why Bechor? Rabbi Akiva's Example of a Consecrated Animal Outside the Temple
The Bechor's Status: Permitted to Slaughter with a Permanent Blemish Outside the Temple
The Halachic Question: What Happens to Bechor Skin If Treifa Is Discovered After Flaying?
Ruling: Halacha Like Rabbi Akiva for Bechor Outside the Temple
Caveat: Expert Verification of the Blemish Is Required
Final Ruling: Halacha Like the Sages for Korbano: Invalidity Timing Matters

topical_tags.term: piggul (invalid sacrifice due to wrong intention)
zrikat hadam (sprinkling of blood)
hefsheta (flaying/skinning)
kohanim skin entitlement (who gets the skin)
Rebbi (Rabbi Judah the Prince) - blood permits skin independently
Rabbi Eliezer ben Shimon - blood only works for meat, not skin alone
Rabbi Eliezer - korban remains valid even without meat
Rabbi Yehoshua - no blood without meat (im ein basar ein dam)
Rabbi Akiva - invalidity discovery timing determines skin status
Rabbi Chanina Sgan Kohanim - never saw skin burned separately
tamei (ritually impure)
mizbeach (altar) entitlement to meat
bechor (firstborn animal)
treifa (mortally wounded/defective animal)
beit hasreifa (burning place outside temple)
lechatchila bediavad (prospectively forbidden but retroactively valid)
absence of evidence is not evidence
olah (burnt offering - meat goes entirely to altar)
kedusha (sanctity of offering)
mum (permanent blemish)
discovery of invalidity (nimtzat treifa)
